text: DONOGHUE v STEVENSON (1932). COURT: House of Lords (United Kingdom). FACTS: Mrs. Donoghue consumed a bottle of ginger beer purchased by a friend at a café in Paisley, Scotland. The bottle contained the remains of a decomposed snail. She became ill and sued the manufacturer, Stevenson. Because she had not purchased the bottle herself, she had no contractual relationship with the manufacturer. HOLDING: The House of Lords held that a manufacturer owes a duty of care to the ultimate consumer of its products, even in the absence of a contractual relationship. Lord Atkin articulated the NEIGHBOUR PRINCIPLE: 'You must take reasonable care to avoid acts or omissions which you can reasonably foresee would be likely to injure your neighbour.' SIGNIFICANCE: (1) Established the modern law of NEGLIGENCE and the DUTY OF CARE. (2) Lord Atkin's NEIGHBOUR PRINCIPLE is the foundation of tort law across the common law world. (3) One of the most important cases in common law history.

citation: R v Dudley and Stephens (1884) 14 QBD 273
text: R v DUDLEY AND STEPHENS (1884). COURT: Queen's Bench Division. FACTS: Dudley, Stephens, and Brooks were shipwrecked with a cabin boy, Richard Parker, and adrift in a lifeboat for 20 days without food or water. Dudley and Stephens killed and ate Parker to survive. They were rescued and charged with murder. HOLDING: The Court held that necessity is NOT a defence to murder. Dudley and Stephens were convicted of murder (later commuted to six months' imprisonment). REASONING: Lord Coleridge held that the deliberate killing of an innocent person cannot be justified by necessity. The absolute prohibition on murder cannot be qualified by a utilitarian calculus of lives saved versus lives lost. SIGNIFICANCE: (1) Established that NECESSITY IS NOT A DEFENCE TO MURDER in English law. (2) Foundational case in criminal law and legal philosophy. (3) One of the most discussed cases in legal education worldwide.

ENTICK v CARRINGTON (1765). COURT: Court of Common Pleas. FACTS: Officers of the King entered John Entick's house under a warrant issued by the Secretary of State, Lord Halifax, and seized his papers. Entick sued for trespass. HOLDING: Lord Camden held that the warrant was unlawful because there was no statutory or common law authority for the Secretary of State to issue general warrants to search private property. REASONING: Every invasion of private property is a trespass unless it can be justified by some positive law. Executive power is limited by law. 'If this is law it would be found in our books, but no such law ever existed in this country.' SIGNIFICANCE: (1) Foundational case for the RULE OF LAW and protection against arbitrary state power. (2) Influenced the Fourth Amendment to the US Constitution. (3) Established that executive authority must have legal authorization.

case_name: Carlill v Carbolic Smoke Ball Company
citation: Carlill v Carbolic Smoke Ball Co [1893] 1 QB 256
text: CARLILL v CARBOLIC SMOKE BALL CO (1893). COURT: Court of Appeal (England & Wales). FACTS: The Carbolic Smoke Ball Company published an advertisement offering £100 to anyone who contracted influenza after using their product as directed. Mrs. Carlill used the smoke ball as directed and contracted influenza. She sued for the £100. The company argued the advertisement was mere puffery and not a binding contract. HOLDING: The Court of Appeal held that the advertisement was an offer to the world at large, which Mrs. Carlill had accepted by performing the conditions. The company was bound to pay. SIGNIFICANCE: (1) Foundational case in CONTRACT LAW — unilateral offers, acceptance by performance, certainty of terms. (2) One of the most widely taught contract law cases. (3) Established that advertisements can constitute binding offers in certain circumstances.

case_name: R (Miller) v Secretary of State for Exiting the European Union
court: Supreme Court of the United Kingdom
citation: R (Miller) v Secretary of State for Exiting the EU [2017] UKSC 5
text: R (MILLER) v SECRETARY OF STATE (Brexit Case, 2017). COURT: Supreme Court of the United Kingdom. FACTS: The UK Government sought to trigger Article 50 of the Treaty on European Union to begin the process of withdrawing from the EU (Brexit) using the royal prerogative, without an Act of Parliament. HOLDING: The Supreme Court held (8-3) that the Government could not trigger Article 50 without an Act of Parliament. Withdrawal from the EU would change domestic law by removing rights that Parliament had enacted through the European Communities Act 1972, and the Government could not use prerogative power to alter rights granted by statute. SIGNIFICANCE: (1) Affirmed PARLIAMENTARY SOVEREIGNTY — the government cannot use prerogative powers to override or frustrate Acts of Parliament. (2) Landmark constitutional case on the balance of power between Parliament and the Executive. (3) Most significant UK constitutional case in decades.

case_name: Associated Provincial Picture Houses Ltd v Wednesbury Corporation
citation: Associated Provincial Picture Houses v Wednesbury Corporation [1948] 1 KB 223
text: WEDNESBURY CASE (1948). COURT: Court of Appeal. FACTS: Wednesbury Corporation granted a license to the plaintiff cinema to show films on Sundays, subject to a condition that no children under 15 be admitted. The cinema challenged this condition as unreasonable. HOLDING: Lord Greene MR held that courts could intervene to quash a decision of a public authority only if the decision was so unreasonable that no reasonable authority could ever have come to it (WEDNESBURY UNREASONABLENESS). SIGNIFICANCE: (1) Established the WEDNESBURY UNREASONABLENESS test — the foundational standard for judicial review of administrative decisions in English law. (2) Set the threshold for judicial intervention in executive decisions. (3) One of the most cited cases in UK administrative law, now being supplemented by proportionality analysis.
